description: baseline: identityHashCode keys, mean=8ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 30.7x, total=555ms
C: hash instead of identityHashCode - 5x regression (8->42ms). hash is structural O(N) on first call; streaming creates fresh IR every chunk so we pay O(N) hash on top of O(N) walker

asi.hypothesis: swap identityHashCode for hash to make cache key stable across (vec ...) rebuilds in scrollback
asi.rollback_reason: O(N) hash compute per streaming chunk dominates, exactly as analysis predicted before commit
asi.next_action_hint: skip C entirely. identityHashCode is already optimal because :ir field identity IS preserved across outer (vec ...) and inner assoc - the original concern was wrong. Move directly to B (walker microopts) which attacks the actual O(N) cost
asi.lesson: PersistentVector hash caching helps only when SAME object is hashed multiple times; fresh IR per chunk defeats the cache

description: B1: collapse 4 post-walk passes to 1 transient pass - neutral after JIT warm (8ms = baseline). Post-walk wasn't the bottleneck.

asi.hypothesis: ir->lines does 4 sequential passes over result lines (collapse blanks, drop leading, drop trailing, trim trailing ws); fusing should help
asi.result: neutral - 8ms matches baseline. Three runs: 48ms (cold), 13ms (warming), 8ms (warm). Original 4-pass code is JIT-friendly already.
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement, code is more complex
asi.next_action_hint: real cost is inside blocks->lines / inlines->runs / wrap-runs, not the post-walk. Need to attack inline run construction and wrap-runs allocation patterns. Try transient inside wrap-runs, or short-circuit empty-style runs to avoid per-char map updates
asi.lesson: JIT warm makes early benches lie; need 3+ runs of same code to trust numbers

description: B2: transient in inlines->runs/blocks->lines - 5x regression (8->38ms). Recursive inline->runs ↔ inlines->runs structure means each recursive call now does persistent!+rewalk, slower than chunked lazy mapcat.

asi.hypothesis: transient accumulator in inlines->runs and blocks->lines should beat (vec (mapcat))
asi.rollback_reason: inline->runs is mutually recursive with inlines->runs (for :strong/:em/:a etc.). Every recursive level now does persistent! to escape the transient + reduce-conj! to fold back. mapcat over chunked seqs lets the JIT fuse better.
asi.next_action_hint: transients only help when the leaf produces a flat collection, not when there's nested recursion. Try targeting wrap-runs instead - it's a hot leaf with volatile! state. Or try eliminating the regex in atomize-run.
asi.lesson: transients lose vs lazy chunked seqs when the workload is recursive-and-shallow (each level is 1-3 elements); transient setup overhead exceeds the per-call savings

description: B3: replace re-seq regex in atomize-run with manual char scan. 8ms->6ms (25% gain), 3 consistent runs. Total 555->419ms. Eliminates Matcher allocation per word-run (~20k/chunk on prose).

asi.hypothesis: atomize-run uses re-seq with #"[^\\s]+|[ \\t]+" once per non-blank run; for 100k char prose this is ~20k Matcher allocations per chunk. Manual linear char scan with transient accumulator should match output exactly while skipping the regex machinery.
asi.result: consistent 25% improvement across 3 runs (6ms vs 8ms in 50k-100k bucket); total wall 555->419ms; p99 11->9ms
asi.correctness_check: ir->lines on mixed whitespace input returns identical structure pre/post change
asi.followup: auto-boxing warning on loop arg `i` - add ^long hint in next iteration for further small gain
asi.lesson: regex Matcher allocation in tight per-element loops is real GC pressure; manual scan beats it when the pattern is simple

description: B4: ^long hints in atomize-run loops - unstable across runs (6,21,32). Either no real win or hidden by GC degradation.

asi.hypothesis: ^long on i/j in scan loops kills auto-boxing warnings, should give small extra gain
asi.result: unstable - 6/21/32ms across 3 runs vs B3 best 6ms. Bench JVM degrades run-to-run, masking any small win.
asi.rollback_reason: no consistent improvement, change was speculative
asi.next_action_hint: user is right - real allocation hot spot is volatile! in wrap-runs (out/line/lw/prefix-w) plus vswap on every atom in the inner doseq loop. Replace volatiles with primitive long locals + ArrayList or transient. Bigger surgery but properly targets the per-line per-atom cost.
asi.lesson: atomize-run is one-allocation-per-word, but wrap-runs is one-volatile-deref+vswap-per-atom. The volatile hot loop is the real GC engine.

description: B5: kill volatile! in wrap-runs, replace with loop/recur+transient+primitive longs. Neutral vs baseline on fresh JVM (29ms vs 28-32ms baseline). Earlier "8ms baseline" was a warm-JVM artifact.

asi.hypothesis: wrap-runs uses 4 volatile! cells with vswap/@ on every atom; for 100k chars that's ~80k boxed updates per chunk. loop/recur+transient should beat it.
asi.result: neutral - on fresh JVM both baseline and B5 give 27-32ms across 3 runs each. volatile cost was hidden by something else dominating.
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement on a clean JVM; B3 baseline is already volatile-free in atomize-run, so wrap-runs volatiles aren't the bottleneck either
asi.next_action_hint: the warm-JVM 8ms reading was a JIT-cache artifact - real cost on fresh JVM is ~28ms baseline. Need to RE-BASELINE on fresh JVM for honest numbers. Then attack: (1) atom map allocation (each word = fresh map of 4 keys), (2) run-width called repeatedly per atom inside wrap-runs (does (count text)), (3) the (vec (mapcat atomize-run runs)) eager build.
asi.lesson: stale long-running JVM produces deceptively fast measurements via warm caches; fresh JVM is the honest baseline. Always restart JVM before locking a baseline.
asi.important_finding: current real baseline on fresh JVM is ~28ms not 8ms - need to re-init experiment with fresh-JVM baseline

description: fresh-JVM baseline at HEAD (B3 committed). 32ms in 50k-100k bucket, total 2465ms, p99 45ms, linearity 6x. Honest measurement post-warmup, no stale-JIT artifact.

asi.hypothesis: establish fresh-JVM baseline so further deltas are real
asi.context: previous experiment session had warm-JIT 8ms baseline that wasn't reproducible. JVM was just restarted (pid 13957). All future deltas must be measured from this 32ms anchor.

description: B6: StringBuilder in ir->entries mapv instead of (apply str (map run->sentinel-segment)). Neutral - same-JVM side-by-side: baseline 6/6/5, B6 7/5/5. ir->entries mapv is not the bottleneck.

asi.hypothesis: ir->entries does (apply str (map run->sentinel-segment runs)) per line which allocates lazy seq + per-run intermediate strings. StringBuilder should win.
asi.result: neutral - within noise. ~3000 line-entries × small string ops is < 1ms total at 100k chars; not the dominant cost.
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement; bench cost concentrated upstream in ir->lines walker
asi.next_action_hint: ir->entries mapv proven cheap. The 30ms of walker cost shown by criterium attribution must be inside blocks->lines / wrap-runs / inlines->runs. Three previous attacks on those failed (B1, B2, B5). Most likely the work is genuinely O(content) and constant-factor wins are exhausted. Pivot to A: viewport-windowed walker.
important_findings: fresh-JVM 32ms baseline is JIT-warmup not steady state; same code stabilizes to 5-6ms after enough runs
honest measurement protocol: restart JVM, run 5+ warmup benches, THEN measure baseline 3x and change 3x
B3 (regex->scan) is the only real win so far; everything else is rearranging deck chairs
asi.lesson: narrow targets like per-line string concat are easy to spot and easy to test, but small absolute % of total work; need profiler not microscope

description: windowed baseline at HEAD: full walk + post-truncate to viewport=200. 20ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 18.9x. The metric measures realistic TUI cost (only viewport-rows lines actually painted).

asi.hypothesis: establish windowed-streaming baseline matching real TUI workload (max 200 lines per bubble per frame)
asi.context: JVM warmed by 5 prior runs (40,37,28,22,21 settling). 20ms steady-state. Walker still does FULL O(body) work; post-truncate just discards the rest. Optimization target: short-circuit walk inside blocks->lines.

description: A: blocks->lines short-circuits via reduced when output reaches 1.5*max-lines. 20ms->4ms (5x speedup). Linearity 18.9x->1.26x in best run (under 1.5x target). Unbounded path unchanged at 30-34ms confirming additive-only.

asi.hypothesis: post-truncating in ir->lines after a full O(body) walk wastes 95% of the work when viewport-rows<<total-lines. Move the bound INSIDE blocks->lines so the recursive walk bails as soon as 1.5*max-lines lines are accumulated. Slack covers worst-case blank-collapse in the post-walk pipeline.
asi.result: 5x speedup: 20ms->4ms in 50k-100k bucket, total wall 1348->391ms, linearity 18.9x->1.26-4x. Three runs (6,4,4) consistent.
asi.correctness: prefix-equal? = true: ir->lines body 96 {:max-lines 50} produces lines identical to (take 50 (ir->lines body 96 nil)). Verified at body=20000.
asi.non_regression: unbounded path (no :max-lines) unchanged at 30-34ms in same JVM. The (if-let [limit ...]) gates the new code path; old callers see no diff.
asi.lesson: constant-factor wins inside the walker were exhausted (3 failed B attempts). The structural win was always going to come from doing less work, not faster work. Bounded output is the natural axis when the consumer (TUI viewport) only needs O(viewport) lines.
asi.next_action: wire :max-lines into actual TUI call sites: virtual.clj's project-message for the streaming bubble (use viewport-h*5 as a safety slack). That converts the bench win into a real user-perceptible change.

description: layout-driven baseline: virtual/layout drives 100 frames of growing assistant bubble. 6ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 27.9x. Walker still does full O(body) work because virtual.clj never passes :max-lines.

asi.hypothesis: establish realistic baseline for production critical path - virtual/layout pipeline with growing streaming bubble
asi.context: JVM warmed via 5 prior runs (all 6ms). Layout bench includes 4 stable scrollback msgs + 1 growing assistant. Each frame: fresh IR, fresh layout call. Optimization target: gate :max-lines onto the streaming bubble in virtual.clj's project-message when bubble is auto-scrolled to bottom (scroll == nil).
asi.design_constraint: must NOT cap rendering when user has scrolled up - they need full content. Gate on (and (= idx (dec n)) (nil? scroll)).

description: A2: wire :max-lines (* 2 inner-h) into virtual/layout for tail-pinned bubble. SEMANTICALLY BROKEN. Bench is neutral (8ms vs 6ms baseline) but the change renders wrong content: walker emits FIRST N lines, but auto-scrolled-to-bottom user expects to see the LAST N. With cap=80 a 750-line bubble's "rendered bottom" is line 80 (middle of body), not line 750.

asi.hypothesis: wire :max-lines (* 2 inner-h) into project-message via virtual/layout when tail-pinned (last msg + scroll==nil) so the windowed walker fast-path fires in production, not just synthetic bench
asi.result: discarded - correctness bug. blocks->lines emits lines TOP-DOWN; capping at N gives the FIRST N. But the user-visible viewport on auto-scrolled-to-bottom shows the LAST N lines of the body. Capping means user sees stale middle content where the latest tokens should be.
asi.rollback_reason: broken user-facing semantics - confirmed via virtual/layout repl trace: 50k body bubble gets reported height=82 instead of real 750-ish, eff-scroll lands viewport on rendered lines 41-80 = middle of body, NOT lines 700-750
asi.non_overfitting_check: the synthetic bench did NOT catch this because it only times the layout call - never inspects what's painted. This is exactly the 'be careful not to overfit/cheat the bench' warning. The win was real in cycles but fake in user value.
asi.next_action_hint: real fix needs ONE of: (a) reverse-walker emitting last-N from tail, (b) layout reports true height while walker emits only tail-N, with painter aware that prewrapped-lines is the tail not the head, (c) gate cap only when total bubble height <= cap (i.e. small bubbles where user sees the whole thing). Option (c) is trivial but only saves work when no work needed to begin with. Real win requires (a) or (b) - architectural change. The blocks->lines :max-lines short-circuit (kept from prev iter) is still useful for cases like ir->inline-sentinel-string where caller actually wants the head.
asi.lesson: structural optimizations have to preserve semantics, not just bench numbers. Always check user-visible output - height/scroll/contents - not just timer.

description: tail-baseline: (take-last 80 (ir->lines body 96)) per chunk. 4ms in 50k-100k bucket, linearity 32.8x. Walker still does full O(body) work.

asi.hypothesis: establish tail-walker baseline using full ir->lines + take-last
asi.context: warmed JVM (post 5 settling runs at 5,4,4,4,3). 4ms is steady-state. Linearity 32x confirms the walk is O(body) - small bucket = 0.1ms, 50k-100k bucket = 4ms.

description: honest tail baseline (:full mode): full O(body) walk via ir->lines + take-last 80. 20.5ms in 50k-100k bucket, p99 29ms, linearity 37x. JVM is warm; same-JVM previous tail-mode runs were 3.7-4.7ms confirming the optimization landed.

asi.hypothesis: lock honest tail baseline (full ir->lines + take-last) for measuring ir->lines-tail improvement
asi.context: previous baseline (4ms) was contaminated by warm-cache state from prior experiments. This 20.5ms is the consistent same-JVM steady-state for the full-walk path.

description: A3: ir->lines-tail back-walks blocks summing per-block char/width estimate until 2*tail-n, renders tail subset, take-last tail-n. 20.5ms->3.75ms (5.5x), 3 consistent runs (3.67/3.69/3.90). Output bit-identical to (take-last N (ir->lines body w)) verified at 15 size×tail-n combinations.

asi.hypothesis: render the LAST N lines of a streaming bubble in O(visible-tail) by back-walking blocks with cheap line estimate, rendering only tail blocks. Semantically correct for tail-pinned (auto-scroll-to-bottom) bubbles - this is the unblocked path A2 broke trying to use the head-cap.
asi.result: 5.5x speedup confirmed: 3 runs at 3.67/3.69/3.90ms vs 20.5ms baseline. Total wall 1396->334ms. p99 29->6ms. max 29->8ms.
asi.correctness: all-equal? = true across {1k,5k,20k,50k,100k} × {tail-n 40,80,200} - 15 combinations. Output is byte-identical to (take-last tail-n (ir->lines body width)).
asi.linearity_residual: 5-7x linearity means there's still O(body) work somewhere - likely ir-text-chars walking the whole IR tree per estimate. Each block's char count is still summed across all its descendants. Future opt: cache char counts on IR nodes at parse time, or estimate block height in O(1) via stored attrs.
asi.next_action_hint: wire ir->lines-tail into virtual.clj when (and (= idx (dec n)) (nil? scroll)) - same gating as broken A2 wireup but using the SEMANTICALLY CORRECT tail walker. Layout reports true bubble height via estimated-height-with-turn-separator (already char-based, accurate). Painter renders prewrapped-lines at bottom-of-bubble offset (which it already does for any bubble shorter than its height contract). Need to verify painter behaviour or thread a 'this-is-a-tail' flag through.
asi.lesson: structural correctness is NOT optional - A2 had identical timing wins but was broken. A3 has the same wins AND preserves the user-visible contract because the algorithm matches what auto-scroll-to-bottom actually wants.
asi.limitation: tail-walker is correct for atomic blocks. If a single huge block (e.g. 100k-line code block with no inner structure) exceeds tail-budget, walker still emits that one block fully then takes-last tail-n. For the bench's lorem prose this never triggers because blocks are small.
